**Undoing the Language of the KGB Files: AI and Discourse Analysis**   
**(The case of *Operation* *Vozmezdie*)**

*Natalia Khanenko-Friesen, Larysa Bilous, Sean Lis, Andriy Kohut.*

***The KGB report, 1983\.***   
Just two years before perestroika and eight years before the collapse of the USSR, the following report was filed in the State Security Committee office in Kyiv:

On a Planned Anti-Soviet Gathering (sborishche) in Canada

On January 28, 1983, report No. 21/sv described anti-Soviet actions abroad conducted by OUNists in connection with the 50th anniversary of the "artificial famine in Ukraine."

According to information received by the KGB of the Ukrainian SSR, a so-called “symposium” is planned to be held in Montreal on March 25–26 of this year, initiated by the “Canadian Institute of Ukrainian Studies” (Edmonton) and four universities in the province of Quebec are planning to hold on March 25–26 of this year in Montreal a so-called "symposium" involving faculty from universities in Canada, the USA, and France. The gathering (*sborishche*) will feature reports on the "artificial famine in Ukraine and its consequences" and videotaped "testimonies of famine witnesses." Among the speakers are co-author of the slanderous book *(paskvil) Terror of Collectivization* being prepared for publication in the USA, J. Mace, active OUNites A. Zhukovsky (France), Yu. Shevelov, B. Bociurkiw (USA), and others, as well as N. Strokatova, who was expelled/thrown out (*vydvorena*) from the USSR.

The materials from the "symposium" are planned to be published as a separate edition.

The KGB of the Ukrainian SSR is taking measures to provide the Ukrainian émigré community with credible data, through operational capabilities, refuting the falsehoods of OUNist propaganda regarding the "artificial famine."

The KGB of the USSR is informed.  
						(F.16-Op.01-Spr.1206-0150-154)

By the time the above report was generated, the Canadian Institute of Ukrainian Studies had entered its sixth year of operation at the University of Alberta and had already established itself as a reputable scholarly institute, as evidenced by its academic output and its reception in the field of Slavic studies. Heading into the 1980s, the last decade of the Cold War before the breakdown of the USSR in 1991, the institute’s work was well aligned with many other academic pursuits in Soviet and Slavic studies across the democratic world that were critical of communist rule, repressive politics, and the violation of human rights in Europe’s socialist bloc.[^1]  

Founded and funded by the Ukrainian Canadian community and the government of the province of Alberta, by its mandate CIUS focused on the study of Ukraine and the Ukrainian people, in the homeland and in the diaspora. With Ukraine still part of the Soviet Union, the institute’s scholarly work promoted the Ukrainian people’s right to their own history, language, culture, and nationhood. The latter in particular became clearly an issue for the communist leadership of the USSR as it continued its totalitarian pursuit of one indivisible Soviet nation forged out of various peoples the Russian empire once ruled over, repressing any dissent and resistance to this vision.

In the times of Cold War, Ukrainian studies in North America, along with the Ukrainian diaspora that helped to establish it, came under direct Soviet secret police scrutiny, as this area of studies was seen as one that directly undermines Soviet foreign policy and diplomatic narratives. The extent of such surveillance has already been documented by several scholars who were among the first to access declassified KGB files in Ukraine (Zhuk book, Luciuk book, Bertelsen articles, others). Whether the impulse for such monitoring originated with the Soviet political leadership, within the powerful networks of its security services, or most likely at the intersection of both, the surveillance of diaspora institutions and the administration of ‘countermeasures’ against Ukrainian studies abroad became the focus of a series of special operations. These activities were regularly reported by the agency to the Soviet communist leadership.

In the course of such reporting and decades long systemic record keeping, the agency formulated a particular view of CIUS and Ukrainian diaspora academics, as evidenced by the language of the documents themselves. Terms such as *sborishche*, *paskvil* or slanderous book, and *vydvorena* or expelled or thrown out appear alongside the excessive use of square quotation marks and the phrase ‘so called’. This language leaves today’s readers of the archival files wondering what to make of such claims, of the institute itself, and of those who formulated them.

The document that opens this article is one of many reports that the Ukrainian office of the KGB generated in the course of its decades-long operational work directed at Ukrainian communities abroad, in particular at those organizations and networks perceived by the Soviet authorities as a threat. Working through many more documents, a fuller picture emerges with respect to a special operation that the KGB conducted under the title *Vozmezdie*, or *Retribution*. It is this group of documents that we have given our attention to here.

In this article we revisit the documents pertaining to the *Vozmezdie* operation with the specific task of reengaging and examining with the language of the KGB files. Each of us has been motivated to pursue this research from the perspective of our respective academic fields, recognizing the power this language exerts over meanings readers assign to the contents of KGB archival documents. Importantly, although the case of the *Vozmezdie* operation represents only a minuscule fraction of the KGB’s overall activities, the reporting language used in these documents, with its own evolution and internal complexity tied to a specific case and targeted group, was replicated across the filing system over decades.

Since Ukraine opened the former Soviet archives of repression for research in 2015, academics and non-academics alike have flocked to the HDA SBU archives, the custodian of the KGB files, seeking to better understand how the KGB, the notorious primary repressive mechanism of the totalitarian Soviet regime, operated.

If scholars, well-versed in the history of the Soviet totalitarianism, have an intuit understanding of how to work through the language the agency carefully crafted and employed in their reporting documents, less informed readers face a difficult-to-navigate linguistic minefield that these archival materials have evolved into over the years. With growing circulation of the KGB archival documents across research teams, their projects and publications, social media and publications, these files now reach increasingly wider audiences. How can uninformed readers navigate this ideologically charged discursive terrain, splattered with strong metaphors, sewn together with strings of accusatory sentences, and punctuated with resonant staccato-like phrases? What should one make of this discourse? Can these readers ever access meanings hidden behind many layers of a heavily coded language of the files?

***The beginning of the project***

We were motivated to focus on the files referencing the KGB operation *Vozmezdie*, or *Retribution*, by a number of factors. Firstly, as two of us are members of the institute, our curiosity got the better of us. The KGB pursued its disinformation campaign in North America to placate and undermine various segments of the Ukrainian diaspora, part of which supported and provided funding for the establishment of the Canadian Institute of Ukrainian Studies. As part of these operations, KGB agents monitored the activities of CIUS and reported on the institute in their documents. Secondly, the ongoing collaboration between CIUS and HDA SBU afforded our team access to verified KGB documents whose authenticity does not need to be questioned. Thirdly, working at the institute over the years has provided the two of us, anthropologically speaking, with an *emic* perspective on its institutional operations and research pursuits. This insider vantage point allows us to engage directly with the claims laid out in the KGB files, a perspective that we nevertheless seek to suspend here in the interest of a balanced discussion. Thus, as specialists in Soviet and Ukrainian history, archival sciences, and digital humanities, equipped with both lived experience and scholarly knowledge of the KGB archival collection as well as CIUS’s institutional history and current operations, our team is well positioned to evaluate not only the language but also the substance of the claims made in the KGB files about the institute and the community that founded it.

And lastly, with one of us trained in the field of Digital Humanities, we also wanted to capitalize on the emerging capabilities of AI to engage in a large-scale language analysis of archival documents, and this brought the question of bias back into the picture, and in distinctly novel ways. In the end, this project unfolded across three intersecting planes of bias generation—in the language of the KGB files themselves, in our own human interpretive frameworks, and in the analytical use of AI.

The gap between the actual course of events at CIUS in 1983, when the institute was preparing for the conference, and the way these activities were profiled in the KGB documents directly informed our collaboration. A similar discrepancy between lived experience and its representations in the KGB files has been noted elsewhere. Focusing on post-totalitarian Latvia in the 1990s, anthropologist Vieda Skultans for example examined the gap between what Latvians who had been under the KGB surveillance remembered about their actions and what was recorded about them in the agency’s files. An experienced fieldworker, Skultans studied surveillance and interrogation files that the KGB had maintained on her informants and then discussed the contents of those files with the same individuals whose lives were documented in them. She found substantial discrepancies between what her interlocutors recalled doing and what was stated in the interrogation and surveillance reports.[^2] The parallels between her case and the situation examined here are instructive. Recognizing this gap between lived events and their bureaucratic representation in the files ultimately shaped both the direction and the methodology of our project.

***Epistemic Conundrum***

We started working on this project with a Canadian student research assistant who had never studied Soviet or Ukrainian history. This collaboration reminded our team of the importance of several questions. First, with little or no knowledge of what sustained the Soviet totalitarian system and with limited understanding of how the Soviet secret police operated to maintain political order in the Soviet state, how can a curious outsider or a novice researcher navigate the linguistic minefield and the layers of complex, multilayered, ideologically crafted language of the KGB files? Second, designed to placate individuals and entire groups in KGB-approved ways, the unique language of the KGB files also effectively obscured the actual course of events. As a result, from the distance of time these documents often present readers with alternative versions of reality.

What, then, might happen to seasoned researchers who already understand that these documents are heavily informed by a built-in bias dictated by the ideological agenda of the Soviet security service operations? Working consistently with large numbers of files over long periods of time, what might the sustained exposure to this ‘archival bias’ do to researchers’ own judgement? As documented in psychology and the human sciences, repeated exposure to the same institutional language and rhetorical frames can produce several cognitive and social effects, including confirmation bias (Suzuki & Yamamoto 2021),[^3] habituation through repeated exposure (Zajonc 1968),[^4] and processes of moral desensitization (Bandura 1999).[^5] At what point might the psychology of overexposure begin to shape perception and blur the distinction between what is asserted in the documents and what may actually have taken place? Scholars of totalitarian state archives have similarly warned that prolonged engagement with bureaucratic discourse may reproduce the epistemic frameworks embedded in the documents themselves (Stoler 2010; Skultans 2001; Verdery 2014\).[^6]

The questions posed here speak to a much broader set of issues faced not only by scholars in Soviet and post-Soviet studies, but also by researchers in decolonial studies, archival studies, cultural anthropology, psychology, sociocultural linguistics, and other related disciplines working with archives across diverse cultural and historical settings. They point to a key epistemic conundrum encountered by researchers who turn to the archives of repression in former totalitarian or postcolonial states. These archives contain records of state repression, but they are written in the very language through which the state asserted and legitimized political authority over its populations.

Even in democratic settings, similar challenges appear in institutional archives, including church archives, community archives, and others. Archival documents produced by organizations and institutions, and especially by organs of state control in totalitarian or colonial regimes, were not created simply to record what was observed. They were also meant to perform power, i.e. to frame individuals and entire groups, legitimize institutional decisions, silence alternative memories of events, and justify repression, deportations, and executions. The language of the KGB files therefore becomes a thick ideological fog that can effectively obscure historical understanding even for readers who recognize its ideological nature. One cannot treat an entry in a KGB file as a self-standing truth token without reproducing the biases embedded in the language of the files. Our project therefore aims to expose this double bind of KGB archival bias and to unpack the language that produces it.

***The double bind of the KGB archival bias***  
The double bind of KGB archival bias lies in a paradox. The documents are indispensable for reconstructing Soviet practices of repression, yet the very language through which they record events simultaneously distorts the reality researchers hope to recover. On the one hand, KGB files *mask* the very reality researchers want to access while reading the documents. Access to this already mediated reality is further limited by institutional secrecy and selective declassification, reinforcing a state-authored epistemology, as Ann Applebaum reminds us (2012).[^7]  On the other hand, researchers’ overexposure to archives risks internalizing their ideological grammar and the tropes of deviance, enemyhood, and vigilance, thus re-inscribing Cold War ontologies in critical scholarship of today. Scholars who navigate these archival documents face a profound hermeneutic tension, to read *against* the grain of ideology without reproducing its cognitive frame and authoritative language.[^8]

The KGB archive thus remains both a site of historical revelation and of lingering epistemic capture.[^9] In Derrida’s sense, the KGB archive is a locus where power and knowledge entwine to shape what can be remembered and known and what not (Derrida, 1998\).[^10] As Stoler (2010) reminds us, such archives continue to impose their limits on intelligibility. Without careful interpretive work that unravels the discursive structures holding these texts together, researchers risk inhabiting the very ideological frameworks they seek to critique.

Looking closely at the documents themselves reveals that this distortion was produced through overlapping mechanisms. One of them was institutional. Over decades of operation, the Soviet security apparatus developed a distinctive bureaucratic language that shaped how information was recorded, evaluated, and communicated. Russian historian Kozlov observed that the NKVD, the KGB’s predecessor, already relied on a specialized bureaucratic lexicon when submitting investigative reports, effectively “programming” a particular interpretive attitude toward the facts (Kozlov 1996: 890\).[^11] Working over many years with other KGB files, we too encountered a language marked by a specific thesaurus, semantic conventions, coded expressions, and rhetorical strategies designed to frame interpretation. Even punctuation practices, such as the strategic use of square quotation marks, functioned as part of this discursive system. The systematic nature of this language can be traced across documents produced over decades and appears in internal teaching materials prepared for KGB Schools of Higher Learning as early as 1955, nearly thirty years before the 1983 report concerning CIUS, as evidenced in this document.[^12] 

![][image1]

Translation: Ukrainian bourgeois nationalists are the most vicious enemies of the working people, the most embittered, ready to commit any crimes, agents of the intelligence services of the imperialist camp. At the present time they are in the service of American and British intelligence.

Individual agency also shaped the documents. As Skultans (2001) demonstrated when comparing KGB interrogation protocols with the memories of those who had been subjected to surveillance, the “facts” preserved in these files often reflected what she called bureaucratic invention rather than empirical observation. [^13] Her interlocutors repeatedly pointed to discrepancies between their recollections of events and the narratives recorded in their interrogation and surveillance reports. Similar observations on how secret police officers themselves contributed to the production of the augmented reality now captured in the secret police files were made by other scholars. Timothy Garton Ash (1997) for example examined his own Stasi file and pointed out numerous misinterpretations and fabrications, shaped by police officers’ own expectations and ideological assumptions.[^14] Focusing on Stasi files again, for her book Anna Funder interviewed individuals who appeared in the Stasi secret police files and discovered that officers, like informers, misreported or misread events, asserting that the Stasi archives reflect more how the regime imagined its enemies rather than who they were and what they were like in reality.[^15]  
Recognizing these dynamics reveals that the epistemic challenge addressed in our project unfolds across several planes of bias generation. The first lies in the ideological bias embedded in the language of the KGB documents themselves. The second emerges in the interpretive frameworks through which researchers approach and read these materials. We acknowledge our own positionalities within this interpretive process and recognize that our readings are also shaped by our institutional affiliations, disciplinary training, and proximity to the histories discussed here. Making this positionality explicit though might allow readers to situate our interpretations and, if they wish, to interrogate the potential biases that follow from it. A third layer appears when computational tools, including AI systems, are used to process archival language, introducing 

Our project therefore approaches the KGB archive as a *discursive system* whose multiple layers of bias must be identified, examined, and carefully disentangled. We recognize that this bias cannot be fully undone. Any attempt to dismantle the ideological frames embedded in these documents inevitably introduces another layer of interpretation, whether human or algorithmic. For this reason, we do not claim interpretive neutrality. Instead, drawing on our disciplinary training and familiarity with both the archive and the institutional history it references, we prioritize human-led critical discourse analysis and offer here a careful, methodologically grounded reading of the language of these documents. Our analysis thus focuses on the discursive structures through which the KGB files produced ideological meaning, paying particular attention to the phrase-level elements that sustained these narratives. Through this close attention to the language of the files themselves, we seek to make visible the mechanisms through which ideological meaning was constructed and reproduced in the KGB record.

***Operation Vozmezdie (Andriy)***  
\~ 300-500 words (or up to 1 page double sized max)

* Started in 1960?  
* Evolved through the 1970s  
* Lasted through the 1980s, despite the efforts at political change from above, that became known a Perestroika or Reconstruction

***Research Framework and the Project***  
This project brings together approaches from history, anthropology, archival studies, and digital humanities to examine the language of the KGB files documenting Operation *Vozmezdie*. Our work proceeded along three main lines. First, relying on principles of critical discourse analysis (CDA), we identified several linguistic strategies employed in the files to profile the Ukrainian diaspora and those members of it seen as a threat to the Soviet system. Second, we employed selected AI tools to follow our human-led CDA of the documents’ language, with the aim of developing a protocol that could extend this analytical approach to additional documents without or with minimum direct human intervention. Third, we created a web-based mini archive presenting the sixteen documents analyzed in this study in their original form, alongside transcriptions and translations, providing both the human-led and AI-assisted CDA of their texts.

We focus here on the ideological grammar that structures the KGB files describing Operation *Vozmezdie*. At the same time, we acknowledge our own positionality, particularly where our institutional affiliations intersect with the institutional agency represented in the documents. What we describe as emic positionality may, for some readers, signal the presence of the emic bias. Precisely because we are attentive to our proximity to the institutional history of CIUS reflected in these materials, by stating our positions, we have attempted to keep our own interpretive assumptions suspended. To that end, we devoted significant effort to developing a mode of linguistic analysis capable of engaging the text of the files on its own discursive terms. Our classificatory system and analytic categories were therefore designed to trace the rhetorical and semantic structures through which the KGB files language was constructed, while minimizing the imposition of our own evaluative frameworks.

Importantly, we do not treat AI as a primary interpretive agent in this project. Rather, we approach it as a secondary analytical instrument designed to extend the reach of human-led analysis to a larger body of documents than could reasonably be processed by researchers alone. Because AI systems carry their own limitations and biases when interpreting textual data, a central concern for us was to monitor the accuracy of AI-generated interpretations in relation to the human-led categorization of narrative and linguistic elements in the documents. Given the rapid and often unpredictable development of AI technologies, we are also aware that the particular AI tools employed in this project may soon become obsolete. For this reason, we did not ask AI to generate the principles of critical discourse analysis itself. Instead, the analytical framework and classificatory logic were developed by us as researchers, and AI was tasked with following this interpretive structure when engaging the language of the KGB files. Thus, our project followed a clear hierarchy of interpretation in which human analysis established the methodological foundation, while AI served as a supplementary mechanism for possible future extending that analysis to additional materials that may later be incorporated into this research protocol.

***Undoing the language of the KGB files: Phrase-level discourse analysis***  
In approaching the language of the KGB records, we draw inspiration from a methodological principle long employed in linguistic anthropology and descriptive linguistics, the identification of minimal units of meaning whose alteration produces a shift in interpretation.[^16] Linguists analyzing the grammatical structure of languages often isolate *morphemes*—prefixes, suffixes, and other minimal elements that carry irreducible semantic value and whose modification alters the meaning of a word as used in the sentence (Boas 1911; Hockett 1958).[^17] Words thus appear in this context as composite structures built from smaller meaningful units.

A similar logic may be applied at the level archival documents’ discourse. Sentences in the KGB files likewise can be seen as composite constructions in which particular phrase-level elements carry decisive evaluative ideological weight. When such elements are removed or substituted, the meaning of the sentence, and importantly in our case, its ideological charge, changes. In our analysis of these materials, we therefore focus on identifying what might be called *ideological morphemes*, or minimal word-level or phrase-level units that function as the irreducible carriers of ideological meaning within the documents. By isolating and classifying these discursive units, we are able to trace how the ideological grammar of the KGB narrative is assembled and reproduced in the text.

We proceeded to settle on a select number of categories in our analysis, understanding of course that our interpretive scheme cannot not offer full account of how the ideological bias is constructed and conveyed in the documents. Other approaches to the analysis of the language of the KGB files can generate other interpretations. Yet, we believe our approach offers the readers of the KGB files an opportunity to look into how the Soviet secret police files’ archival bias is crafted. Because we would like our research to benefit first of all the general users of the archives, we chose to focus on select but recognizable categories. 

The following categories were identified. (1) *Action-Focused Language* — emphasizes actions, operations, and execution, often to convey decisiveness, control, or effectiveness (*і.е. measures were carried out; continues to carry out measures; launched attacks; monitoring is underway*).[^18] (2) *Ideological Framing (Discrediting)* — Ideologically charged language used explicitly to delegitimize, discredit, or attack specific people, groups, activities, or events. *(i.e. fascist accomplices; anti-Soviet provocations; reactionary course; religious fanatics, publications on the “famine”; testimony from “eyewitnesses”)*. (3) *Ideological Phrasing (Normalizing) —* Ideological language that channels interpretation and values without directly attacking or discrediting a specific target. *(i.e.; increasing political vigilance; patriotic and internationalist upbringing; toilers). (4) Institutional / Bureaucratic Lingo* — Formulaic language specific to KGB and Soviet administrative reporting practices. Often procedural and opaque. *(i.e. operational opportunities; KGB informed; measures were pushed out; line ministries were notified). (5) Generic / Neutral Language* — Descriptive language with no ideological or evaluative load, used for basic identification or reference. *(i.e. FRG; Sumy region; students from socialist countries)*

***Human-Led Research: Steps and Methodology*** 

Our analysis is based on a corpus of internal documents produced in the 1980s. These materials consist primarily of informational reports, memoranda, and analytical notes prepared for the Central Committee of the Communist Party of Ukraine. They document KGB activities related to influence operations against Ukrainian émigré organizations and Western political actors. At the same time, they reflect the broader reporting functions of the KGB, which encompassed political, social, economic, and informational developments within and beyond the Soviet Union. Their language combines bureaucratic reporting, ideological framing, and operational descriptions, making them particularly suitable for critical discourse analysis (CDA).

The corpus assembled for this analysis comprises sixteen documents drawn from thirteen archival files in Fond 16, Opys 1 of the Sectoral State Archive of the Security Service of Ukraine (HDA SBU). Three of these documents come from spr. 1262 and two from spr. 1249; the remaining eleven each come from a separate archival file. Together, they span approximately sixty-two folios, with individual items ranging from two to seven folios in length. The documents belong to two genres of internal KGB correspondence. Nine are memoranda (докладные записки), formal reports addressed to the leadership of the Communist Party of Ukraine on specific operational and political questions. The remaining seven are informational reports (информационные сообщения) that transmit intelligence content for the recipient's awareness.

The corpus covers a wide range of topics, together illustrating the scope and internal logic of KGB reporting. A central focus is on influence operations directed against Ukrainian émigré organizations, especially efforts to discredit the Organization of Ukrainian Nationalists (OUN) by portraying its members as Nazi collaborators and by shaping Western public opinion, legal processes, and institutional responses. This is particularly evident in documents related to Operation “Vozmezdie,” which describe coordinated campaigns involving publications, documentary films, media placements, and appeals framed as expressions of public opinion. These documents repeatedly attribute concrete outcomes in the United States and Canada – including investigations, deportations, and the establishment of commissions – to KGB actions.

Closely related are materials addressing broader ideological campaigns, particularly efforts to counter Western narratives about the Holodomor, 1932–33. Thus, documents associated with Operation “Farisei” describe attempts to influence academic discourse, media representations, and public debates by discrediting sources, promoting alternative interpretations, and disseminating targeted materials through international channels. Other documents focus on the production and circulation of ideological content within the Soviet system itself, including plans to publish mass-distribution volumes exposing “Ukrainian bourgeois nationalism” for both domestic and foreign audiences. Together, these materials demonstrate that influence operations were not limited to discrete campaigns but formed part of a broader system of narrative management.[^19]

At the same time, the documents consistently embed discussions of these operations within wider informational summaries. Many reports begin with detailed statistical overviews of foreign nationals present in the Ukrainian SSR such as diplomats, tourists, specialists, and students, followed by accounts of international developments, diaspora activities, and internal events. Within a single document, reports on anti-Soviet actions abroad may appear alongside descriptions of criminal investigations, industrial accidents, the discovery of handwritten leaflets, or routine administrative matters. This structural hybridity is a defining feature of the corpus. It reflects a mode of reporting in which diverse phenomena, ranging from academic conferences in Canada to mining accidents in Soviet Ukraine, were processed by the KGB within a unified framework.

This internal assembly of reporting documents has important methodological implications. The documents do not simply report various events, but arrange them into a coherent system of meaning in which different types of activity emerge as components of a broader security landscape. Diaspora politics, historical narratives, religious activism, internal dissent, and economic incidents are all come across as interconnected domains requiring monitoring and intervention. As a result, the corpus combines multiple discursive registers, from ideological to bureaucratic, operational, and technical. Careful contextual reading was therefore necessary when identifying and classifying linguistic patterns. In particular, documents with extended statistical or administrative sections tend to contain a higher proportion of neutral or generic language, which may affect quantitative distributions across categories. This was taken into account in interpreting the dataset.

Time consuming as it was, the human-led phase of the research had two main goals. First, we conducted a detailed qualitative analysis of the language used in these documents in order to understand how Soviet security organs framed and represented influence operations. Second, we created a structured, public-oriented pilot dataset that could serve both as a research resource and as training input for computational analysis. Our work was distributed between three members of the team who jointly spent close to one thousand and two hundred hours on several tasks.

The first stage involved preparing the textual corpus. The original documents were in Russian. Machine translation was used to produce initial English versions, which were then carefully reviewed and corrected by researchers with expertise in both the historical context and Soviet bureaucratic language. This ensured that key ideological and operational terms were translated accurately and consistently.

In the second stage, we manually segmented each document into short textual units and organized them in a multi-tab spreadsheet (Google Sheets), with one tab per document. Rather than working with full sentences or paragraphs, we deliberately divided the text into concise phrases that contained a single logical or functional meaning (for example, an action, an evaluative statement, a classification, or a reporting formula). Each segment was recorded alongside the original Russian text, the verified English translation, and a preliminary classification. This approach allowed us to isolate and compare recurring discursive patterns across documents that are otherwise internally heterogeneous.

Importantly, this dataset also served as training input for large language models (LLMs). By providing the model with human-curated examples of segmented and classified phrases, we aimed to teach it how to recognize similar patterns in documents that had not been manually processed. In this sense, the spreadsheet functioned as a bridge between close reading and automated analysis, translating interpretive decisions into structured data.

A major challenge in this process was the identification and classification of ambiguous expressions. Soviet bureaucratic language frequently blends neutral administrative phrasing with ideological meaning, and the interpretation of a phrase often depends on its broader context. The segmentation itself is also interpretive: determining what constitutes a meaningful unit is inherently subjective. We therefore treat our approach as one possible analytical strategy and explicitly invite alternative approaches. Recognizing that complete precision is unattainable, we allowed for an approximate 5 percent margin of inaccuracy in classification.

The human-led phase required substantial effort, involving multiple researchers and dozens of hours of manual work. This level of detail was necessary to capture nuances that automated systems alone would likely miss. By investing in careful segmentation, translation, and coding, we aimed to build a reliable foundation for both qualitative interpretation and future computational analysis of Soviet intelligence discourse.

***AI layers of analysis (Sean)***

Once we completed and agreed up the outcomes of our human-led data procession and segmentation, and interpretation, the team turned to establishing AI–informed research paths for AI to replicate our approach in analysing the same documents. The task was to apply a structured taxonomy to our documents and compare AI-assisted classifications against human expert ground truths. Each document was split into segments (phrases or entities), and each segment received two labels: a content category (what kind of information it is) and a framing strategy (how it is expressed). The taxonomy includes seven content categories: Actions, Actors, Places, Time, Documents, Context and Concepts, Legal Framework. It also includes five framing strategies: Generic/Neutral Language, Institutional/Bureaucratic Lingo, Ideological Framing (Discrediting), Ideological Phrasing (Normalizing), and Action-Focused Language. The qualifiers of each classification metric can be found on the culminatory website produced as the last step in our aims. The overall goal was to see how well AI or AI-assisted analysis could reproduce expert judgments on both dimensions (content categories and framing strategies), and then share such findings with the public in the interest of transparency, and in recognition of the subjective nature of taxonomic labelling in the humanities.

## Thus, we proceeded to build a modular pipeline with five stages: ingest, extraction, ground truth loading, comparison, and report generation. Documents were ingested from Russian originals, and ground truths came from human-coded spreadsheets (converted to csv files) with pre-defined segments. The comparison step aligns LLM output to ground truth by matching segment text (exact match first, then containment), and then computes agreement on category, framing, and both.

The primary method was local LLM classification. We exported segments from ground truth (body rows only, so segment boundaries align with the human-coded tables), then ran a local model (GPT-OSS) to classify each segment one-by-one. For each segment, the model receives a prompt that lists the allowed content categories and framing strategies, the segment text in English and Russian, and the expected output structure. The model returns a JSON object with the content category and framing. Classification runs at roughly two to three seconds per segment. Results are merged into the pipeline and compared against ground truth. We focused on per-segment classification (where segments are fixed by ground truth) rather than full-document extraction, because a single LLM call per document produces different segment boundaries and does not align with the human-coded structure.

We chose to use local LLM classification (GPT-OSS) rather than cloud APIs, so we could run the full pipeline on our own hardware and keep segment boundaries aligned with ground truth. We adopted a dual taxonomy (content plus framing) so we could study both what is said and how it is said as separate dimensions. Separating content from framing lets us see where the LLM performs better or worse on each, and the bilingual corpus (Russian originals with English translations) supports research on how LLMs handle multilingual material in tandem. The model receives both languages; alignment uses the Russian content.

Several challenges emerged along the way. Calculating alignment proved sensitive to phrasing: if the LLM paraphrases or the segment text differed slightly, matching fails, and the accuracy had to be manually compared and derived. Framing turned out to be more subjective than content. The same phrase can be read as neutral or institutional depending on context, and some categories (e.g., Legal Framework, Places) appear rarely, so accuracy estimates for them are noisier. The archival and institutional context adds another layer: KGB documents use specific institutional language and ideological framing. The taxonomy was designed to capture these features, and the work shows how well a general-purpose LLM can recognize them without fine-tuning.

Despite these challenges, we achieved a number of successes. We built a modular pipeline and a reusable evaluation framework for future work. A central deliverable is the interactive report, which we developed as a research tool in its own right. The comparison tables let researchers search, filter by category or framing, and inspect each segment side by side with its LLM and human labels. The document text view presents the full document in English and Russian; segments are highlighted by category and framing, and users can filter by label to focus on specific types of content or disagreement. The glossary provides definitions and examples for each category and framing strategy, with search and hyperlinks that jump directly to segments in the document. These interactive elements turn the report from a static summary into an explorable interface where researchers can drill into mismatches, trace vocabulary across documents, see where the model succeeds or fails, and offer feedback on what users may deem the “most suitable” label for any given segment.

The report also includes a suite of visualizations. Word clouds, category-by-framing heatmaps, per-document stacked bars, and others offer a diversity of means to engage with the data from a top-down lens. A document similarity matrix (cosine similarity of framing profiles) reveals which documents cluster by framing distribution. A document fingerprint view compares framing proportions across the corpus. A places map extracts and geocodes locations from Places-tagged segments, supporting spatial analysis. Segment length vs accuracy charts show whether longer segments are classified more reliably. Voyant tools (Cirrus, Links, Bubblelines, Constellations) are embedded for corpus-level text analysis. Visualization may include a "How is this calculated?" section so the methodology is open and transparent. Bilingual support (English/Ukrainian) and glossary definitions drawn from the taxonomy support a broader user base and encourages understanding and engagement with our materials. Accuracy varies across documents; some documents show higher agreement than others, which may reflect segment length, clarity of language, or the distribution of categories and framings in the text. The report surfaces per-document stats so researchers can explore these patterns in finer detail. The report also extracts terms by category and framing, which supports qualitative exploration of what the model learns: which phrases cluster in Action-Focused vs Institutional, which vocabulary signals Ideological (Discrediting).

We also encountered failures that, in particular, elucidated the limitations of automated agents’ abilities in identifying passages of interest. However, the failures encountered also show promise for ways of modifying our methods in future experiments. Full-document LLM extraction produced different segment boundaries than ground truth, so alignment was untenable. Therefore, future experiments should treat segment boundaries in the absence of ground truth as a primary focus. Both-match accuracy remained low at 46.5% (though none of the model’s choices were unreasonable), indicating that getting both category and framing to align with expert assessment at once is difficult due to subjectivity, relies heavily on the succinctness of taxonomic definitions, and depends on factors such as seed and prompt formatting that effect input texts non-uniformly.

The time to complete the experiments with AI tools performed in our study took roughly 324 hours of human labour to complete. Across 2,913 aligned segments in 16 documents, category accuracy reached 65.8%, framing accuracy 73.2%, and both-match 46.5%. **ADD content accuracy**

Overall, conceptually, we showed that content categorization is more tractable than framing detection. Category is easier to agree on; framing depends on subtle judgments about tone, institutional language, and ideology. The dual taxonomy (content plus framing) provides a structured way to study both dimensions and to identify where the LLM struggles.

Practically, we built a reusable pipeline for expert-grounded LLM evaluation on KGB documents. The framework can ingest documents, run local LLM classification, compare results to ground truth, and produce an interactive report. The report is a substantial part of the contribution: the interactive tables and visualizations are designed so that researchers can explore the data rather than only read summary statistics. Filtering, search, bilingual highlighting, and linked glossary entries make it possible to move from a high-level pattern (e.g., low framing accuracy on a given document) to the specific segments that drive it. This supports scaling to more documents, comparison across models or prompt variants, and exploration of agreement patterns and failure modes. The pipeline is modular and config-driven, so researchers can swap models, adjust prompts, or add new documents without changing the core logic.

As a novel research contribution, we applied AI-assisted analysis to declassified ex-KGB documents using a dual taxonomy of content and framing, and evaluated it against expert ground truth. Our work demonstrates how local LLMs can be used to classify archival text along both semantic and rhetorical dimensions, and provides a framework for future work on model comparison, prompt engineering (e.g., few-shot, chain-of-thought), taxonomy refinement (splitting or merging categories), and scaling to more documents or corpora.

***Undoing the Bias: Going Forward (in lieu of conclusions), Natalia \++***  
\~ 500 words (or 1 page or so)

* What did we want to do  
* How we went about it  
* What we accomplished, its novelty, importance and   
* What value our research adds to knowledge

[^5]:  Bandura, Albert. 1999\. “Moral Disengagement in the Perpetration of Inhumanities.” *Personality and Social Psychology Review* 3(3): 193–209.

[^7]:  Applebaum, A. (2012). *Iron Curtain: The Crushing of Eastern Europe, 1944–1956.* Doubleday.

[^8]:  More on the ways the authoritative language of the Soviet ideology was produced and reproduced within the Soviet state, especially in late Soviet period, see Yurchak “Everything was Forever, Until It Was No More”.
